text:
In this study, we embark upon an exegetical and theological examination of the last pericope of the Book of Sirach: the Greek version of Sir 51:13-30. The structure of the text falls into two parts: the autobiographical confession of the sage in the first section (vv. 13-22) and the didactic section in the second part (vv. 23-30). The sage recounts his pursuit of wisdom in the first section and it must be noted that his search for wisdom occurs throughout his whole life, commencing in his youth and persisting until the end. In his narrative of the quest for wisdom he lists the means that the sapienti has undertaken throughout his life and in order to obtain wisdom these means are prayer, cult, the Law, purity of heart and skills of the mind.

Part 2: The Educator In this section, the Teacher, as a master of knowledge, invites the ignorant to come and participate in the knowledge he has acquired in his "house of instruction" (school). This section of Sirach presents a very optimistic view of the access to wisdom; a view that stands in stark contrast to the views of Job and Ecclesiastes. This article claims that Sir 51:13-30 can be seen as a theological summary of the whole book of Sirach. The section is seen to mirror the opening chapter of the book (Sir 1:1-10) and therefore acts as a literary inclusio, highlighting the connection between wisdom, fear of the Lord and the Mosaic Law.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
